text: The surgeon identifies the uterus and visually locates the inner iliac artery, external iliac artery, and internal iliac artery within the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the surgical field to reveal the deep uterine vein. On the right side, the anterior ligament of the gluteus medius is visible, and the surgeon prepares to pull this ligament.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and notes its position relative to the uterine artery, which is located behind it. The ureter is visible in the surgical field, with the surgeon pointing out its location multiple times.

The surgeon increases the space in the surgical field to avoid visual conflict, focusing on the area in front of the ureter. The ureter is identified and manipulated, while the pararectal space is opened for further access.
The surgeon uses forceps to grasp the paralytic tissue, stretching it to reveal the underlying structures. The ureter is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and separates an artery from a vein, possibly the superior physical artery or rectal artery, using careful dissection techniques.

The surgeon manipulates two anatomical structures, preparing to lift the uterus and make an incision that encompasses two-thirds of the fibroid's volume. The fibroid appears disjointed, and the surgeon adjusts the view for better visibility. The technique involves altering blood flow to the fibroid, which can lead to the demise of smaller fibroids.
The surgical team gently pulls on the tissue, ensuring not to apply excessive tension to avoid tearing. The tissue appears ischemic, indicated by its whitish color, suggesting effective tightening.
